snippets.title: Eigenvalues and eigenvectors with NumPy
snippets.code:
import numpy as np

A = np.array([[4.0, 2.0], [1.0, 3.0]])
eigenvalues, eigenvectors = np.linalg.eig(A)

for i, lam in enumerate(eigenvalues):
    v = eigenvectors[:, i]
    residual = np.linalg.norm(A @ v - lam * v)
    print(f"lambda={lam:.4f}, ||Av - lambda v||={residual:.2e}")
snippets.explanation: `np.linalg.eig` returns eigenvalues and column eigenvectors. The residual ||Av - λv|| should be near machine precision, confirming Av = λv.

snippets.title: Power iteration for the dominant eigenvalue
snippets.code:
import numpy as np

A = np.array([[0.6, 0.4], [0.3, 0.7]])
v = np.array([1.0, 1.0])

for _ in range(50):
    v = A @ v
    v = v / np.linalg.norm(v)

lam = (v @ A @ v) / (v @ v)
print("estimate:", lam, "exact:", np.linalg.eigvals(A).max())
snippets.explanation: Repeatedly multiply by A and normalize converges to the eigenvector with largest |λ| (when eigenvalues are well-separated). Rayleigh quotient estimates λ.

snippets.title: Covariance eigenvectors (PCA preview)
snippets.code:
import numpy as np

# rows = samples, cols = features
X = np.random.randn(200, 2) @ np.array([[2, 1], [0, 0.5]])
X_centered = X - X.mean(axis=0)
C = (X_centered.T @ X_centered) / (len(X) - 1)

evals, evecs = np.linalg.eigh(C)  # symmetric: real orthonormal evecs
print("variances along eigenvectors:", evals)
snippets.explanation: `eigh` is preferred for symmetric covariance matrices; eigenvalues are variances along principal directions.
